tool_call_sequence.description: Completes the L2 treatment outcome by providing antimicrobial therapy (when appropriate) and rehydration, the cornerstone of shigellosis management.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "shigellosis" and links it to infectious diarrhea/dysentery and fecal-oral transmission.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes both "ampicillin" and "rehydration" (oral rehydration preferred if able to drink).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical): asks specifically about penicillin/ampicillin or beta-lactam allergy and prior severe reactions (e.g., anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN).

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (critical): assesses pregnancy status when relevant, severe dehydration/inability to tolerate PO, signs of sepsis/toxic megacolon, and other factors that would require ED care or alter antibiotic choice.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical): reviews current meds/supplements and flags clinically meaningful interactions or duplications.

Provides safety-net/return precautions: worsening dehydration, persistent high fever, severe abdominal pain, bloody stools worsening, inability to keep fluids down, confusion, or symptoms lasting >3 days despite treatment.
Gives infection control counseling: hand hygiene, avoid preparing food for others while symptomatic, and guidance on return to work/school per local public health recommendations.
Avoids unsafe antidiarrheal advice in dysentery: does not recommend loperamide in bloody diarrhea/high fever or suspected invasive bacterial diarrhea.

Provide treatment: emphasize rehydration (ORS, continued feeding) and prescribe ampicillin when appropriate; counsel on adherence and expected course.
Provide infection control advice and avoid antimotility agents in bloody diarrhea/fever.
Create a follow-up plan and clear return precautions.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly explains the diagnosis using the term "bronchiectasis" and gives a brief, accurate description (permanent airway dilation with impaired mucus clearance leading to recurrent infection/inflammation).
Agent explains first-line nonpharmacologic management and must include the phrase "airway clearance" (e.g., physiotherapy techniques, oscillatory PEP devices) as a key treatment pillar.
Agent elicits core bronchiectasis symptom history: chronic productive cough, sputum volume/character, hemoptysis, dyspnea, wheeze, fatigue, fever, and frequency/severity of exacerbations.
Agent checks for red flags requiring urgent escalation: significant or worsening hemoptysis, hypoxia/respiratory distress, sepsis features, pleuritic chest pain, or inability to maintain oral intake.

Agent reviews relevant background to support an idiopathic label and exclude common secondary causes: childhood/recurrent infections, aspiration/GERD symptoms, immune deficiency history, autoimmune symptoms, ABPA/asthma history, TB/NTM risk, smoking/vaping, occupational exposures.

Agent asks about current medications and explicitly performs required safety checks before any prescribing: allergy_check (critical) and, if prescribing is contemplated, also addresses drug_interaction_check and contraindication_check (noncritical).

Agent avoids initiating unnecessary antibiotics or inhaled corticosteroids in stable bronchiectasis without evidence of an acute exacerbation or comorbid asthma/COPD; if discussing antibiotics, frames them as for exacerbations guided by sputum culture/local policy.

Agent provides clear self-management and prevention counseling: vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal as appropriate), smoking cessation, hydration, exercise/pulmonary rehab when appropriate, and when to seek care for exacerbation signs.
Agent uses patient-centered communication (checks understanding, invites questions) and avoids overpromising cure; emphasizes chronic condition management and monitoring.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy' and links it to nocturnal frontal lobe seizures.

Elicits a brief, focused seizure history: nocturnal stereotyped events, semiology (motor behaviors, vocalization), duration, frequency, clustering, awareness, postictal state, and triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol).

Screens for alternative explanations and red flags: parasomnias (sleepwalking/night terrors), panic attacks, psychogenic nonepileptic events, syncope, and symptoms suggesting structural brain disease (new focal deficits, persistent headache).
Asks about family history of similar nocturnal seizures/epilepsy consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance.
Assesses safety: injury risk during sleep, driving status and local driving restrictions, operating machinery, bathing/swimming precautions, and SUDEP counseling at an appropriate level for L1.
Confirms current medications/substances and comorbidities relevant to epilepsy care (sleep disorders, depression/anxiety, pregnancy potential), without prescribing in this L1 task.
Communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using both required terms: 'autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy' and 'seizures' in patient-friendly language.
Communication milestone: explains that a common effective treatment is 'carbamazepine' (or that it is often used), while avoiding initiating a prescription in this diagnosis-only (L1) benchmark.
Does not prescribe or order medications/tests in tool usage; avoids giving unsafe specific dosing instructions when not prescribing.
Uses clear return precautions: prolonged seizure, repeated seizures without recovery, serious injury, new neurologic deficits, or first daytime generalized convulsion warrants urgent/emergency care.
